title: Filter Delay
summary: Three independent delays, each with its own filter — sends different frequency ranges to different delay times. Creates organic, frequency-dependent echo patterns. Best for complex textural effects.
steps: Add Filter Delay to an atmospheric or pad track, or a return track
Each of the 3 bands has: a HP (high-pass) and LP (low-pass) filter to isolate a frequency range, a delay time (sync or ms), and a feedback amount
Enable only the bands you want — each band has an on/off toggle
Example setup: Band 1 (lows, LP 200Hz) = long delay (1/2 note). Band 2 (mids, HP 200Hz LP 3kHz) = medium delay (1/4 note). Band 3 (highs, HP 3kHz) = short delay (1/8 note)
Each band's feedback controls how many times that frequency range repeats
Dry/Wet: 30–50% for a blend of the original with the multi-band delay
tips: Filter Delay creates complexity because different frequencies echo at different rates — the low-end repeats don't line up with the high-end repeats, creating an organic, shifting texture
Start with just Band 3 (highs only) — the high-frequency echoes are most audible and musical without muddying the mix
Used sparingly in transitions: a pad going into a breakdown with Filter Delay has its frequency ranges echoing at different rates as it fades — creates a complex dissolve effect
This device is more complex than Simple Delay. Learn Simple Delay first. Use Filter Delay when you specifically want frequency-dependent delay times
practice_hint: In a breakdown section, add Filter Delay to a chord pad. Enable only Band 3 (highs). Set delay time to 1/8, Feedback 20%, HP filter at 2kHz. This adds a high-frequency shimmer echo without cluttering the mid-range.
ableton_version_note: Available in all editions of Live 9+.
